cards.title: Antenatal Care — Trimester Schedule
cards._legacyHtml:
VisitWeeksKey AssessmentsScreening / Tests
1st Visit (Booking)8–12 weeksConfirm pregnancy (USS dating), medical/drug/family history, BP, weight, BMIFBC, blood group & Rh, rubella, syphilis (VDRL), HBsAg, HIV, MSU, cervical smear if due
2nd Visit16 weeksFundal height, FH auscultation, BP, urine dipstickAFP (if not done at booking), discuss Down's screening results
3rd Visit20 weeksAnomaly USS, fundal height, BP, fetal movements enquiryAnomaly scan (detailed USS — cardiac, NTD, renal)
4th Visit25 weeks (primips)Symphysis-fundal height (SFH), BP, urine, fetal movementsFBC (anaemia screen)
5th Visit28 weeksSFH, presentation, BP, urineGTT (gestational diabetes), FBC, Anti-D (Rh-ve mothers)
6th Visit31 weeks (primips)SFH, presentation, BP, urineReview 28-week results
7th Visit34 weeksSFH, presentation, BP, urineFBC, Anti-D 2nd dose (Rh-ve)
8th Visit36 weeksSFH, presentation (confirm cephalic), BPGroup B Strep swab (if local protocol)
9th Visit38 weeksSFH, engagement of head, BP, urineDiscuss birth plan
10th Visit40 weeksSFH, BP, CTG if indicated, fetal movementsDiscuss post-dates policy / induction
Post-dates41 weeksBiophysical profile, CTGMembrane sweep, induction planning
🟡 Red flags in antenatal care
Refer urgently: BP ≥140/90 (pre-eclampsia) · Severe headache + visual disturbance + epigastric pain (imminent eclampsia) · Reduced fetal movements after 28 weeks · Antepartum haemorrhage (any) · Symptoms of DVT/PE · Rupture of membranes before 37 weeks (PPROM) · Threatened preterm labour. Any of these = same-day obstetric assessment.

cards.title: Obstetric Emergencies — Quick Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
EmergencyDiagnosisImmediate Action
EclampsiaSeizure in pre-eclamptic patient (or within 7 days of delivery)Left lateral, O2, IV MgSO4 4g bolus → 1–2g/hr maintenance. IV labetalol for BP >160/110. Deliver baby once stable.
Placenta praevia (major)Painless bright red APH + low-lying placenta on USS. NEVER do VE.Admit, IV access, FBC, cross-match, O2. No vaginal examination. C-section.
Placental abruptionPainful dark APH, tender woody uterus, fetal distress. USS may be normal.IV access × 2, FBC, coag, cross-match. Urgent delivery (SVD or LSCS). DIC management if severe.
Cord prolapsePalpable/visible cord after ROM + fetal bradycardiaCall for help. Push presenting part UP manually. Knee-chest or Trendelenburg position. Emergency LSCS within minutes.
Shoulder dystociaTurtle sign (head retracts after delivery), failure of shoulders to deliverMcRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflex thighs) + suprapubic pressure. Rubin/Woods manoeuvres. Episiotomy. Last resort: Zavanelli.
PPH (primary)Blood loss >500ml within 24hr of delivery (LSCS >1000ml)4 T's: Tone (uterotonic: oxytocin 10u IM/IV) · Trauma (inspect for tears) · Tissue (retained placenta) · Thrombin (DIC). Bimanual compression. Balloon tamponade. Surgical if refractory.
🟡 PPH — the 4 T's
Tone (uterine atony) = 70–80% of all PPH. First-line: Oxytocin 10 IU IM immediately after 3rd stage. Ergometrine (if no hypertension), Carboprost, Misoprostol. Bimanual uterine compression. Uterine balloon tamponade (Bakri balloon). Interventional radiology / B-Lynch suture / hysterectomy as escalating steps. Tranexamic acid 1g IV within 3 hours of delivery reduces mortality.

cards.title: Labour — Stages & Management
cards._legacyHtml:
StageDefinitionDurationKey Management
1st Stage — Latent phaseOnset of regular contractions to 4cm dilationVariable (hours–days). Nullip >mulipMobilise, reassure, analgesia (TENS/paracetamol/entonox), hydration
1st Stage — Active phase4cm → 10cm (full dilation). Rate ≥0.5cm/hr expectedUp to 12hrs nullip, 6hrs multipCTG monitoring (if high-risk), ARM for augmentation, epidural if requested, 4-hourly VE
2nd StageFull dilation → delivery of babyUp to 2hrs nullip (3hrs with epidural), 1hr multipPushing with contractions, positions (upright/lateral reduce perineal tears), FHR monitoring, anticipate shoulder dystocia/PPH
3rd StageDelivery of placenta + membranes30 min with active managementActive management: Oxytocin 10u IM + controlled cord traction. Check placenta complete. Inspect for tears.
4th StageFirst 2 hours postpartum2 hours minimum monitoringObs every 15 min (BP/P/uterine tone/blood loss), skin-to-skin, breastfeeding initiation, check perineum

cards.title: Postnatal Care & Common Issues
cards._legacyHtml:
IssueTimingAssessmentManagement
Baby bluesDays 3–5 postpartumTearfulness, mood lability, anxiety — transientReassurance, support. Resolves within 2 weeks.
Postnatal depressionWithin 12 months (peak 4–6 weeks)Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS ≥13 = screen positive)CBT, social support, antidepressants (sertraline safest in breastfeeding), refer if severe
Postpartum psychosisFirst 2 weeks (peak 2–4 days)Rapid onset, confusion, hallucinations, manic features, risk to mother and babyPSYCHIATRIC EMERGENCY. Admit. Mother-baby unit preferred. Antipsychotics + mood stabiliser.
Breastfeeding difficultiesDays 1–7Latch problems, engorgement, mastitisLactation consultant, proper latch technique. Mastitis: continue feeding + flucloxacillin if not improving in 24hr
Lochia (normal)Up to 6 weeksRed (lochia rubra, 1–4d) → pink/brown (lochia serosa, 4–10d) → yellow/white (lochia alba, 10d–6wk)Reassurance. Offensive smell or fever = endometritis → refer.
Perineal woundPost-deliveryInspect for haematoma, infection, dehiscenceIce packs (24hr), warm baths, analgesia (paracetamol + ibuprofen). Perineal wound breakdown = refer.

cards.title: Naturopathy in Pregnancy — Safe Practice Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Safety in Pregnancy ★
Many herbs and supplements are contraindicated in pregnancy. Always check before prescribing. The first trimester (weeks 1–12) is the most critical period — organogenesis. Any herb with uterotonic, emmenagogue, or unknown safety profile must be avoided.
ModalitySafe in PregnancyNotes
HydrotherapyWarm baths ✅ (not hot — <37.5°C). Tepid showers. Foot baths.Hot tubs and saunas CONTRAINDICATED — raise core temperature → neural tube defects
Massage✅ 2nd + 3rd trimester (gentle effleurage)Avoid deep pressure on legs (DVT risk), avoid sacrum and acupressure labour points in 1st trimester
Yoga✅ Prenatal yoga — all trimestersAvoid: supine positions >20 weeks, deep twists, hot yoga, inversions, intense core work
Acupressure✅ Most points — avoid labour-inducing pointsAVOID: SP6, LI4, BL32, BL60 until term (can stimulate uterine contractions)
Nutrition therapy✅ Essential — the most important interventionSee key nutrients below
NutrientWhy CriticalDose in Pregnancy
Folate/Methylfolate ★Neural tube defect prevention (spina bifida, anencephaly)400–800mcg/day. Start 1 month pre-conception. MTHFR: use methylfolate not folic acid
IronBlood volume expands 50% — iron demand doubles27mg/day. Iron bisglycinate preferred. Check Hb and ferritin each trimester
DHA (Omega-3)Fetal brain and retinal development (3rd trimester critical)200–300mg DHA/day. Algae-based for vegetarians. Safe throughout pregnancy
Vitamin D3Fetal bone development, immune programming, pre-eclampsia prevention2000–4000 IU/day. Target 25(OH)D >75 nmol/L
IodineFetal brain development and thyroid function220mcg/day. Most pregnancy supplements contain iodine — check label
CalciumFetal skeletal development, pre-eclampsia prevention1000–1200mg/day from food + supplement. Split doses for absorption
🟡 Herbs to AVOID in Pregnancy
Absolutely avoid: Blue cohosh (oxytocic), Pennyroyal (abortifacient), Tansy, Mugwort, Wormwood, Rue, Juniper berry (high dose), Sage (high dose), Senna (long-term), Black cohosh (uterotonic), Licorice root (high dose — cortisol effects on fetus). Use with caution: Ginger (safe for nausea — see NVP card), Chamomile (occasional tea — safe), Echinacea (short-term — safe in 2nd/3rd trimester). Red raspberry leaf — traditional use from 32 weeks only (uterine toning). Always consult a qualified perinatal herbalist for complex cases.

cards.title: Nausea & Vomiting in Pregnancy — Evidence Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
NVP Classification
Nausea +/- vomiting in pregnancy (NVP) affects 70–80% of pregnant women. Hyperemesis gravidarum (HG): severe, persistent vomiting + weight loss >5% + ketonuria → hospitalisation often required.
InterventionDoseEvidenceSafety in Pregnancy
Ginger ★250mg ginger extract QID or 1g fresh ginger tea TDS★★★★ Cochrane: equivalent to vitamin B6 for mild-moderate NVP✅ Safe — multiple RCTs in pregnancy
Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) ★10–25mg TDS (max 200mg/day)★★★★ Strong — first-line recommended by ACOG✅ Safe — standard of care
Acupressure PC6 ★Apply firm pressure to Neiguan point (3 finger-widths above wrist crease, between tendons) × 1 min, 3x/day. Sea-Band wristbands for continuous use★★★ Good — Cochrane review: significant reduction in nausea severity✅ Completely safe
Doxylamine + B6Doxylamine 10mg + B6 10mg (Diclegis) — prescriptive in some countries★★★★ FDA-approved combination✅ Proven safe — extensive data
Peppermint aromatherapyInhale 2–3 drops on tissue as needed★★ Moderate✅ Topical/inhalation safe
Protein-first breakfastEat protein within 30 min of waking (eggs, nuts, yoghurt)★★★ Good — stabilises blood sugar (empty stomach worsens NVP)✅ Safe
Small frequent mealsEvery 2–3 hours — never allow stomach to be empty★★★ Good — prevents gastric acid surge✅ Safe
HG
Hyperemesis Gravidarum — When to Refer Refer to hospital if: unable to keep any fluids down >24hrs, weight loss >5% pre-pregnancy weight, signs of dehydration (dark urine, dizziness), ketonuria, Wernicke's encephalopathy risk (confusion, ataxia, nystagmus — thiamine deficiency). Hospital: IV fluids + antiemetics + thiamine 100mg IV. Thiamine must be given before glucose to prevent Wernicke's.
🟡 PC6 Acupressure Technique
Pericardium 6 (Neiguan): 3 finger-widths proximal to wrist crease, between palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis tendons. Apply firm circular pressure with thumb for 1 minute. Alternate wrists. Use Sea-Band acupressure wristbands for continuous stimulation — studies show maintained anti-nausea effect for 4–8 hours. Mechanism: stimulates vagal afferents, modulates 5-HT3 and dopamine in chemoreceptor trigger zone. Completely safe, free, and evidence-based — teach every pregnant patient this technique.

cards.title: Breastfeeding Support — Naturopathic Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Breastfeeding Benefits
Breast milk is the optimal infant nutrition — WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months, continued to 2+ years with complementary foods. Benefits: IgA immune protection, optimal microbiome seeding, reduced obesity/T2DM/allergy risk, bonding, reduced maternal breast/ovarian cancer risk.
IssueAssessmentNaturopathic Intervention
Low milk supply (perceived)Most "low supply" is perceived, not actual. Check: infant weight gain (150–200g/week), wet nappies (6+/day), feeding frequencyFeed on demand (not schedule), avoid formula supplementation (reduces supply), skin-to-skin contact, rest, hydration 3L/day, oats at breakfast
True low supplyConfirmed by infant weight faltering + reduced wet nappiesGalactagogues: fenugreek 2–3g TDS (most used), blessed thistle 390mg TDS, moringa 1 tsp powder daily. Domperidone (prescriptive) if severe
MastitisBreast redness, warmth, hardness, fever, flu-like symptoms. Usually unilateralContinue breastfeeding (most important) or pump to drain. Warm compress before feed. Cold compress after. Cabbage leaves (cold). If not resolving 24hrs → antibiotics (flucloxacillin). Probiotics post-antibiotic
Nipple pain/traumaUsually latch problem. Cracked nipples, vasospasm (Raynaud's)Lanolin cream after feeds. Silverette cups (antimicrobial). Assess and correct latch — refer lactation consultant. Nipple vasospasm: Vit B6 100mg, magnesium, warm compress after feeds
EngorgementPainful, hard breasts — usually days 3–5 postpartumFeed frequently (every 2–3hrs). Hand express before feed to soften areola. Cold compresses between feeds. Cabbage leaves between feeds (anti-inflammatory compounds reduce engorgement)
🟡 Maternal Nutrition in Breastfeeding
Breastfeeding requires 500 extra calories/day — the highest energy demand of any life stage. Key nutrients: DHA 200–300mg/day (passes into milk, essential for infant brain), Vitamin D 4000–6000 IU/day (breast milk is Vit D-poor regardless — infant needs separate supplement), Iodine 290mcg/day, B12 (critical for vegan mothers — deficiency in breast milk causes irreversible infant neurological damage). Continue prenatal supplement throughout breastfeeding. Calcium 1300mg/day. Avoid: high-mercury fish, alcohol (wait 2hrs per standard drink before feeding), excessive caffeine (<200mg/day = 2 coffees).

cards.title: High-Risk Pregnancy — Naturopathic Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ High-Risk Pregnancy — Obstetric Co-Management Is Mandatory
Any high-risk pregnancy condition requires obstetric specialist co-management. BNYS naturopathic role: nutritional optimisation, lifestyle support, stress reduction, complementary symptom management — alongside, not instead of, conventional obstetric care. Never advise against investigations or conventional monitoring in high-risk pregnancy.
ConditionDefinitionNaturopathic SupportEvidence
Gestational Diabetes (GDM)Glucose intolerance first detected in pregnancy. Diagnosed at 24–28 weeks with OGTT (India: 75g — GDM if 0hr≥92, 1hr≥180, 2hr≥153 mg/dL).Low-GI diet: eliminates white rice, refined flour, sugar, fruit juice. Complex carbs only. Protein at every meal. Myo-inositol 4g/day ★★★ (reduces GDM incidence by 60% in high-risk women — multiple RCTs). Exercise: 30 min post-meal walking ★★★ (reduces postprandial glucose spike 22%). Self-monitoring glucose diary. Glyburide or insulin if diet + exercise fail (conventional).★★★ Myo-inositol prevention RCTs
Preeclampsia PreventionHTN (≥140/90) + proteinuria or severe features after 20 weeks. Risk factors: nulliparous, BMI>35, multiple pregnancy, prev PE, CKD, T1DM, antiphospholipid syndrome.Calcium 1.5–2g/day from 20 weeks ★★★ (WHO recommendation — reduces severe PE by 50% in low-calcium-intake populations — highly relevant in India). Aspirin 150mg from 11–14 weeks ★★★ (all moderate-high risk women). Magnesium 400mg ★★ (low Mg associated with PE). Vit D 4000 IU ★★. Low-sodium diet. Supine rest avoidance (improves uteroplacental flow).★★★ Calcium WHO Grade A; Aspirin GRADE A
Preterm Birth PreventionBirth before 37 weeks. Risk factors: prev PTB, cervical incompetence, short cervix, infection, smoking, stress, multiple gestation.Progesterone (17-OHPC or vaginal progesterone) — conventional — if short cervix (CL<25mm) ★★★. Omega-3 DHA+EPA ★★★ (DINO trial + meta-analysis: omega-3 reduces PTB <34 weeks by 42%). Magnesium IV (conventional — neuroprotection if imminent PTB). Periodontal treatment (gum disease associated with PTB — dental care in pregnancy is safe and important).★★★ Omega-3 for PTB prevention
IUGR (Intrauterine Growth Restriction)Fetal growth below 10th centile for gestational age. Causes: uteroplacental insufficiency, infection, chromosomal, maternal malnutrition.Optimise maternal nutrition: protein 1.5–1.8g/kg, iron, folate, zinc, Vit D. Bed rest + left lateral position (improves uteroplacental perfusion). Quit smoking ★★★ (most important modifiable cause). Aspirin (if vascular IUGR — reduces RI on Doppler). Biophysical profile and CTG monitoring (conventional — critical).Clinical optimisation

cards.title: Postnatal Depression & Maternal Mental Health
cards._legacyHtml:
Postnatal Depression — The Most Common Complication of Childbirth
PND affects 10–15% of new mothers (higher in India — up to 23% in some studies). Often unrecognised and untreated. Important to distinguish: Baby blues (days 1–5, transient, normal — 80% of women), PND (weeks to months after birth), and Postpartum psychosis (first 2 weeks, emergency — hallucinations, delusions, confusion → ADMIT immediately).
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
EPA-dominant omega-3★★★ Meta-analysis: omega-3 (particularly EPA 1–2g) significantly reduces PND severity. EPA more effective than DHA for mood.DHA during pregnancy (fetal brain). EPA dominance postpartum (maternal mood). Prescription: EPA 2g + DHA 1g/day from 34 weeks gestation through 6 months postpartum.
Bright Light Therapy★★★ RCT: 7000 lux morning light therapy 30 min/day significantly reduces PND symptoms10,000 lux light box, 30 min within first hour of waking. Position at 30–45° angle. Do not look directly. Duration: 4–8 weeks. Also improves circadian disruption from night feeding.
Saffron★★★ Multiple RCTs: saffron 30mg/day comparable to fluoxetine 20mg for mild-moderate depression (not disease-specific to PND but strong general antidepressant evidence)Saffron extract 30mg/day (stigma and style). Petals less potent than stigmas. 6-week trial. Safe while breastfeeding per limited evidence (consult prescriber).
Yoga + MBSR★★★ Multiple RCTs specifically in postnatal period: yoga reduces PND severity, improves mother-infant bonding, reduces anxietyMother-baby yoga classes ★★★ (social connection + yoga + infant interaction — multiple benefit pathways). Individual home practice if class unavailable. 20–30 min/day. Restorative and gentle initially.
Social support★★★ Most consistent predictor of PND recovery: quality social support. Isolation = strongest PND risk factor.Mother-infant groups, family education, partner involvement, peer support, postpartum doula if available. BNYS practitioners: facilitate warm referrals to community support — this is as important as any supplement protocol.
1
Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) — Screening 10-item self-report questionnaire validated for postpartum depression. Screen at: 4–6 weeks postnatal, 3 months, 6 months. Score ≥13 = probable major depression — refer to physician + psychiatry if indicated. Score 10–12 = monitor closely + naturopathic support. Question 10 (self-harm): any score >0 requires immediate clinical assessment. EPDS is free, translated into multiple Indian languages (Hindi, Kannada, Tamil). Take 5 minutes. Every midwife, ASHA worker, and BNYS practitioner in contact with new mothers should administer it routinely.

cards.title: Menstrual Disorders — Adolescent to Perimenopause
cards._legacyHtml:
Menstrual Disorders — A Life-Span Perspective
Menstrual disorders affect 70% of menstruating women at some point. Effective naturopathic management requires understanding the underlying mechanism — not just the symptom. The same symptom (e.g., irregular cycles) has completely different underlying causes in a teenager (hypothalamic immaturity, PCOS, eating disorder) vs a perimenopausal woman (ovarian reserve decline).
ConditionDefinitionCausesNaturopathic Protocol
Primary DysmenorrhoeaPainful menses with no structural pelvic pathology. Onset within 2 years of menarche. Prostaglandin-mediated uterine cramping.Excess prostaglandin F2α + E2 production. Associated with: omega-6 excess, low omega-3, magnesium deficiency.Omega-3 3g/day ★★★ (prostaglandin substrate shift → less painful PGs). Mg glycinate 360mg from day 15 ★★★. Ginger 250mg QID ★★★ (comparable to mefenamic acid). Heat therapy ★★★ (40°C abdominal heat pad — as effective as ibuprofen). Yoga: gentle inversions, supported poses during menstruation.
Secondary DysmenorrhoeaPainful menses caused by structural pathology. New onset in mid-late reproductive years. Worsening dysmenorrhoea after previously painless periods.Endometriosis (most common), fibroids, adenomyosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, ovarian cysts, IUD (Cu-IUD).Diagnose underlying cause first (pelvic USS + gynaecology referral). Endometriosis: anti-inflammatory protocol (omega-3, curcumin, DIM, Vit D), COC for management (conventional), laparoscopy for staging. Fibroids: reduce oestrogen (DIM, cruciferous, weight loss, reduce alcohol).
Amenorrhoea — HypothalamicNo periods for >3 months due to GnRH pulse suppression. Functional — no structural cause.Energy deficiency (relative energy deficiency in sport — RED-S), stress (↑ CRH inhibits GnRH), underweight. Triad: low energy availability + menstrual irregularity + low bone density.Restore caloric sufficiency ★★★ (most important — target >30 kcal/kg FFM/day). Reduce excessive exercise if relevant. Address psychological component (often eating disorder features). Bone protection: Ca 1000mg + Vit D 2000 IU + K2 (bone loss begins within months of amenorrhoea). Ashwagandha ★★ (reduces cortisol, supports GnRH recovery).
Menorrhagia (heavy periods)Objectively: >80ml blood loss per cycle. Subjectively: soaking pad/tampon hourly for several hours, clots >1cm, anaemia.Fibroids ★ (most common structural cause), adenomyosis, polyps, coagulopathy (Von Willebrand — teenage menorrhagia), PCOS, thyroid disease.Iron ★★★ (all heavy menstruation → check ferritin, treat iron deficiency aggressively). Tranexamic acid (conventional first-line). Vit A (heavy bleeding associated with low Vit A — 25,000 IU × 2 weeks, repeat monthly ★★). Chaste tree 20mg vitex extract ★★ (pituitary LH regulation, shortens luteal phase). Exclude structural causes: pelvic USS. Endometrial biopsy if >40 years or risk factors.

cards.title: Postnatal Care & Common Issues
cards._legacyHtml:
IssueTimingAssessmentManagement
Baby bluesDays 3–5 postpartumTearfulness, mood lability, anxiety — transientReassurance, support. Resolves within 2 weeks.
Postnatal depressionWithin 12 months (peak 4–6 weeks)Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS ≥13 = screen positive)CBT, social support, antidepressants (sertraline safest in breastfeeding), refer if severe
Postpartum psychosisFirst 2 weeks (peak 2–4 days)Rapid onset, confusion, hallucinations, manic features, risk to mother and babyPSYCHIATRIC EMERGENCY. Admit. Mother-baby unit preferred. Antipsychotics + mood stabiliser.
Breastfeeding difficultiesDays 1–7Latch problems, engorgement, mastitisLactation consultant, proper latch technique. Mastitis: continue feeding + flucloxacillin if not improving in 24hr
Lochia (normal)Up to 6 weeksRed (lochia rubra, 1–4d) → pink/brown (lochia serosa, 4–10d) → yellow/white (lochia alba, 10d–6wk)Reassurance. Offensive smell or fever = endometritis → refer.
Perineal woundPost-deliveryInspect for haematoma, infection, dehiscenceIce packs (24hr), warm baths, analgesia (paracetamol + ibuprofen). Perineal wound breakdown = refer.

cards.title: Postnatal Depression & Maternal Mental Health
cards._legacyHtml:
Postnatal Depression — The Most Common Complication of Childbirth
PND affects 10–15% of new mothers (higher in India — up to 23% in some studies). Often unrecognised and untreated. Important to distinguish: Baby blues (days 1–5, transient, normal — 80% of women), PND (weeks to months after birth), and Postpartum psychosis (first 2 weeks, emergency — hallucinations, delusions, confusion → ADMIT immediately).
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
EPA-dominant omega-3★★★ Meta-analysis: omega-3 (particularly EPA 1–2g) significantly reduces PND severity. EPA more effective than DHA for mood.DHA during pregnancy (fetal brain). EPA dominance postpartum (maternal mood). Prescription: EPA 2g + DHA 1g/day from 34 weeks gestation through 6 months postpartum.
Bright Light Therapy★★★ RCT: 7000 lux morning light therapy 30 min/day significantly reduces PND symptoms10,000 lux light box, 30 min within first hour of waking. Position at 30–45° angle. Do not look directly. Duration: 4–8 weeks. Also improves circadian disruption from night feeding.
Saffron★★★ Multiple RCTs: saffron 30mg/day comparable to fluoxetine 20mg for mild-moderate depression (not disease-specific to PND but strong general antidepressant evidence)Saffron extract 30mg/day (stigma and style). Petals less potent than stigmas. 6-week trial. Safe while breastfeeding per limited evidence (consult prescriber).
Yoga + MBSR★★★ Multiple RCTs specifically in postnatal period: yoga reduces PND severity, improves mother-infant bonding, reduces anxietyMother-baby yoga classes ★★★ (social connection + yoga + infant interaction — multiple benefit pathways). Individual home practice if class unavailable. 20–30 min/day. Restorative and gentle initially.
Social support★★★ Most consistent predictor of PND recovery: quality social support. Isolation = strongest PND risk factor.Mother-infant groups, family education, partner involvement, peer support, postpartum doula if available. BNYS practitioners: facilitate warm referrals to community support — this is as important as any supplement protocol.
1
Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) — Screening 10-item self-report questionnaire validated for postpartum depression. Screen at: 4–6 weeks postnatal, 3 months, 6 months. Score ≥13 = probable major depression — refer to physician + psychiatry if indicated. Score 10–12 = monitor closely + naturopathic support. Question 10 (self-harm): any score >0 requires immediate clinical assessment. EPDS is free, translated into multiple Indian languages (Hindi, Kannada, Tamil). Take 5 minutes. Every midwife, ASHA worker, and BNYS practitioner in contact with new mothers should administer it routinely.

cards.title: Menstrual Disorders — Adolescent to Perimenopause
cards._legacyHtml:
Menstrual Disorders — A Life-Span Perspective
Menstrual disorders affect 70% of menstruating women at some point. Effective naturopathic management requires understanding the underlying mechanism — not just the symptom. The same symptom (e.g., irregular cycles) has completely different underlying causes in a teenager (hypothalamic immaturity, PCOS, eating disorder) vs a perimenopausal woman (ovarian reserve decline).
ConditionDefinitionCausesNaturopathic Protocol
Primary DysmenorrhoeaPainful menses with no structural pelvic pathology. Onset within 2 years of menarche. Prostaglandin-mediated uterine cramping.Excess prostaglandin F2α + E2 production. Associated with: omega-6 excess, low omega-3, magnesium deficiency.Omega-3 3g/day ★★★ (prostaglandin substrate shift → less painful PGs). Mg glycinate 360mg from day 15 ★★★. Ginger 250mg QID ★★★ (comparable to mefenamic acid). Heat therapy ★★★ (40°C abdominal heat pad — as effective as ibuprofen). Yoga: gentle inversions, supported poses during menstruation.
Secondary DysmenorrhoeaPainful menses caused by structural pathology. New onset in mid-late reproductive years. Worsening dysmenorrhoea after previously painless periods.Endometriosis (most common), fibroids, adenomyosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, ovarian cysts, IUD (Cu-IUD).Diagnose underlying cause first (pelvic USS + gynaecology referral). Endometriosis: anti-inflammatory protocol (omega-3, curcumin, DIM, Vit D), COC for management (conventional), laparoscopy for staging. Fibroids: reduce oestrogen (DIM, cruciferous, weight loss, reduce alcohol).
Amenorrhoea — HypothalamicNo periods for >3 months due to GnRH pulse suppression. Functional — no structural cause.Energy deficiency (relative energy deficiency in sport — RED-S), stress (↑ CRH inhibits GnRH), underweight. Triad: low energy availability + menstrual irregularity + low bone density.Restore caloric sufficiency ★★★ (most important — target >30 kcal/kg FFM/day). Reduce excessive exercise if relevant. Address psychological component (often eating disorder features). Bone protection: Ca 1000mg + Vit D 2000 IU + K2 (bone loss begins within months of amenorrhoea). Ashwagandha ★★ (reduces cortisol, supports GnRH recovery).
Menorrhagia (heavy periods)Objectively: >80ml blood loss per cycle. Subjectively: soaking pad/tampon hourly for several hours, clots >1cm, anaemia.Fibroids ★ (most common structural cause), adenomyosis, polyps, coagulopathy (Von Willebrand — teenage menorrhagia), PCOS, thyroid disease.Iron ★★★ (all heavy menstruation → check ferritin, treat iron deficiency aggressively). Tranexamic acid (conventional first-line). Vit A (heavy bleeding associated with low Vit A — 25,000 IU × 2 weeks, repeat monthly ★★). Chaste tree 20mg vitex extract ★★ (pituitary LH regulation, shortens luteal phase). Exclude structural causes: pelvic USS. Endometrial biopsy if >40 years or risk factors.
